questionBank.explanation: This is the exact MVT statement.
questionBank.notesSnippet: MVT links average rate to an instantaneous rate.

questionBank.question: If f'(x)>0 on an interval, f is:
questionBank.explanation: Positive derivative means function is increasing.

questionBank.question: A relative max can occur where:
questionBank.explanation: Max occurs when slope switches from positive to negative.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Use first derivative sign changes at critical points.
questionBank.timeTip: Track sign before and after c.

questionBank.question: If f''(x)<0 on an interval, graph of f is:
questionBank.explanation: Negative second derivative means concave down.

questionBank.question: To maximize area with constraints, best AP strategy is:

choices.B: Set derivative equal to 0 after reducing to one variable

questionBank.explanation: Optimization: model objective in one variable, then critical points.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Build objective function then analyze derivative.

questionBank.question: Let f be continuous on [2,4] with f(2)=10 and f(4)=20. Which conclusion is guaranteed by the Intermediate Value Theorem?

choices.A: The equation f(x)=13 has at least one solution in the open interval (2,4).
choices.C: f attains a maximum value on the open interval (2,4).
choices.D: f′(x)=5 has at least one solution in (2,4).

questionBank.explanation: 13 lies between f(2)=10 and f(4)=20, so IVT gives c in (2,4) with f(c)=13 (2012 AB #77 reasoning in 5-1 MVT packet solutions).
questionBank.notesSnippet: IVT needs continuity on a closed interval; it does not locate maxima or derivatives.
questionBank.timeTip: Check that the target number is between the endpoint values.

questionBank.question: Let f be differentiable on (1,10). Suppose f(2)=−5, f(5)=5, and f(9)=−5. Which statement must be true?

choices.A: None of the listed conclusions are forced
choices.B: f has at least two zeros in (1,10)
choices.C: f has at least two zeros and the graph has at least one horizontal tangent in (1,10)
choices.D: f has at least two zeros, at least one horizontal tangent, and f(c)=3 for some c in (2,5)

questionBank.explanation: IVT gives zeros in (2,5) and (5,9); MVT on [2,9] gives f′(c)=0 somewhere; IVT on [2,5] hits every value between −5 and 5, including 3 (1998 #91 solution in 5-1 packet — condensed to four options).
questionBank.notesSnippet: Combine IVT for intermediate values and MVT for average slope.
questionBank.timeTip: List what each theorem needs (continuity vs differentiability).

questionBank.question: For f(x)=x^3−3x^2 on [0,3], which value(s) of c in the open interval (0,3) satisfy the conclusion of the Mean Value Theorem?
questionBank.explanation: f(0)=f(3)=0 so average rate is 0; f′(x)=3x^2−6x=3x(x−2)=0 gives x=0 or x=2; only c=2 lies in (0,3) (1985 BC #13 in 5-1 solutions).
questionBank.notesSnippet: MVT gives f′(c)=(f(b)−f(a))/(b−a); exclude endpoints unless the interval is closed only for f.
questionBank.timeTip: Solve f′(c)=average rate, then restrict to the open interval.

questionBank.question: S(t) is twice differentiable. S(0)=0 m, S(20)=−5 m (north positive). For 0≤t≤20, must there be a time when S(t)=−2 m (2 m south)?

choices.A: Yes — S is continuous and −2 is between S(0) and S(20).
choices.C: Yes — by the Mean Value Theorem on [0,20].
choices.D: No — we need S to be linear between sample times.

questionBank.explanation: Twice differentiable ⇒ continuous; IVT on [0,20] applies because −2 is between 0 and −5 (5-1 worksheet #1a solution).
questionBank.timeTip: State the interval where endpoint values bracket the target.

questionBank.question: With S(30)=7 and S(60)=40 (meters north), S twice differentiable, which average velocity equals (S(60)−S(30))/(60−30)?
questionBank.explanation: (40−7)/30 = 33/30 = 1.1; MVT guarantees v(t)=S′(t) equals this average at some t in (30,60) (5-1 #1b solution).
questionBank.timeTip: Use the same units in numerator and denominator.

questionBank.explanation: First derivative test: + to − ⇒ local max (relative extrema packet 5-4).
questionBank.timeTip: Sketch a number line for f′ signs.

questionBank.timeTip: Link f″ sign to bending direction.

parts.question: Find critical points of f.
parts.finalAnswer: x=1 and x=3
steps: Set f'(x)=0.
Critical points at x=1,3.
parts.scoring: 1 pt derivative, 1 pt equation, 1 pt roots.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Classify each critical point.
parts.finalAnswer: x=1 is local max, x=3 is local min
steps: Use sign chart for f'.
f' changes + to - at x=1 => max.
f' changes - to + at x=3 => min.
parts.scoring: 1 pt sign method, 1 pt x=1 class, 1 pt x=3 class.

JUSTIFY YOUR ANSWERS (b) When is the acceleration positive? negative? JUSTIFY YOUR ANSWERS (c) When is the particle speeding up? slowing down? JUSTIFY YOUR ANSWERS (a) The particle is moving to the right on [–7, –5)  (–1, 5)  (5, 7] because v(t) > 0 on these intervals. The particle is moving to the left on (–5, –1) because v(t) < 0 on this interval. (b) The acceleration is positive on (–3, 2)  (5, 7) because v(t) is increasing on these intervals. The acceleration is negative on (–7, –3)  (2, 5) because v(t) is decreasing on these intervals. t v(t) SOLUTIONS (c) The particle is speeding up on (–5, –3) because v(t) < 0 and v(t) is decreasing on this interval The particle is speeding up on (–1, 2)  (5, 7) because v(t) > 0 and v(t) is increasing on these intervals The particle is slowing down on (–7, –5)  (2, 5) because v(t) > 0 and v(t) is decreasing on these intervals The …

5.1.title: 5-1 to 5-5 Extrema and MVT
5.1.content:
Critical-point workflow

Find f', solve f'=0/undefined, then classify using sign changes.

5.2.title: 5-6 Concavity + 2nd Derivative Test
5.2.content:
Concavity

f''>0 concave up, f''<0 concave down. Inflection where concavity changes.

5.3.title: 5-10 Optimization
5.3.content:
Optimization strategy

Objective function -> one variable -> derivative -> test candidates.
